Astral 的开源安全

Open source security at Astral

astral.sh 作者 vinhnx 分数 354 评论 101 2026/04/09 12:11
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. Astral 构建了全球数百万开发人员依赖和信任的工具。这种信任包括对我们安全态势的信心：开发人员合理地期望我们的工具（以及构建、测试和发布它们的流程）是安全的。以最近的 Trivy 和 LiteLLM 黑客事件为代表的供应链攻击的兴起，让开发人员质疑他们是否可以信任他们的工具。为此，我们希望分享一些用于保护工具的技术，希望它们对以下方面有用：
  2. 我们的用户，想要了解我们如何确保他们的系统安全；其他可能从我们使用的一些技术中受益的维护者、项目和公司； CI/CD 系统的开发人员，使项目不需要遵循非明显的路径或避免有用的功能来维护安全和健壮的流程。
  3. CI/CD 安全 # 我们通过在 GitHub Actions 上运行的广泛 CI/CD 工作流程来维持 Ruff、uv 和 ty 的开发速度。如果没有这些工作流程，我们将很难按照我们要求的速度和信心程度来审查、测试和发布我们的工具。我们的 CI/CD 工作流程也是我们安全态势的关键部分，因为它们使我们能够使关键开发和发布流程远离本地开发人员机器并处于受控、可观察的环境内。 GitHub Actions 对我们来说是一个合理的选择，因为它与 GitHub 紧密的第一方集成，以及对贡献者工作流程的成熟支持：任何想要贡献的人都可以使用我们自己使用的相同流程来验证他们的拉取请求是否正确。不幸的是，这也有一个不利的一面：GitHub Actions 的安全默认设置很差，而像 Ultralytics、tj-actions 和 Nx 这样的安全漏洞都是从 pwn 请求等常见的弱点开始的。以下是我们为保护 CI/CD 流程而采取的一些措施：
  4. 我们在整个 GitHub 组织中禁止了许多 GitHub 最危险和不安全的触发器，例如 pull_request_target 和workflow_run。这些触发器几乎不可能安全使用，并且攻击者不断寻找滥用它们的方法，因此我们根本不允许它们使用。我们对这些触发器的经验是，许多项目认为他们需要它们，但绝大多数的用法最好用特权较低的触发器（例如 pull_request）替换或完全删除。例如，许多项目使用 pull_request_target ，以便第三方贡献者触发的工作流可以在 PR 上留下评论，但这些用例通常可以通过作业摘要来很好地满足，甚至只是在工作流日志中留下相关信息。当然，有些用例确实需要这些触发器，例如确实需要对第三方问题或拉取请求发表评论的任何情况。在这些情况下，我们建议完全保留 GitHub Actions，并使用 GitHub 应用程序（或 Webhook）来侦听相关事件并在独立上下文中执行操作。我们在下面的自动化部分更详细地介绍了这种模式。
  5. 我们要求所有操作都固定到特定的提交（而不是可变的标签或分支）。此外，我们会交叉检查这些提交，以确保它们与实际发布的存储库状态匹配，并且不是冒名顶替者提交。我们通过两种方式做到这一点：首先使用 zizmor 的未固定使用和冒充者提交审核，然后再次使用 GitHub 自己的“要求将操作固定到完整长度提交 SHA”政策。前者让我们快速检查是否可以在本地运行（并防止冒名顶替者提交），而后者是工作流执行的硬门，实际上确保所有操作（包括嵌套操作）都完全哈希固定。启用后者是一项艰巨的任务，因为它也需要哈希固定间接操作使用（我们调用的操作调用的操作）。为了实现这一目标，我们与下游（示例）协调，在整个依赖关系图中进行哈希固定。总之，这些检查增加了我们对工作流程的可重复性和密封性的信心，这反过来又增加了我们对其安全性的信心（在攻击者有能力破坏相关操作的情况下）。然而，虽然有必要，但这还不够：哈希固定确保操作的内容是不可变的，但不会阻止这些不可变的内容做出可变的决定（例如从 GitHub 存储库的版本安装最新版本的二进制文件）。 GitHub 和第三方工具在检测此类不变性差距方面都表现不佳，因此我们目前依靠对操作依赖项的手动审查来检测此类风险。当人工审核确实发现差距时，我们会与上游合作来弥补这些差距。例如，对于内部使用本机二进制文件的操作，这是通过在二进制文件的下载 URL 和加密哈希之间嵌入映射来实现的。该哈希值又成为操作不可变状态的一部分。虽然这并不能确保二进制文件本身是真实的，但它确实确保攻击者无法有效地篡改指向二进制文件的可变指针（例如非不可变标签或版本）。

Original Summary

  1. Astral builds tools that millions of developers around the world depend on and trust. That trust includes confidence in our security posture: developers reasonably expect that our tools (and the processes that build, test, and release them) are secure. The rise of supply chain attacks, typified by the recent Trivy and LiteLLM hacks, has developers questioning whether they can trust their tools. To that end, we want to share some of the techniques we use to secure our tools in the hope that they're useful to:
  2. Our users, who want to understand what we do to keep their systems secure; Other maintainers, projects, and companies, who may benefit from some of the techniques we use; Developers of CI/CD systems, so that projects do not need to follow non-obvious paths or avoid useful features to maintain secure and robust processes.
  3. CI/CD security # We sustain our development velocity on Ruff, uv, and ty through extensive CI/CD workflows that run on GitHub Actions. Without these workflows we would struggle to review, test, and release our tools at the pace and to the degree of confidence that we demand. Our CI/CD workflows are also a critical part of our security posture, in that they allow us to keep critical development and release processes away from local developer machines and inside of controlled, observable environments. GitHub Actions is a logical choice for us because of its tight first-party integration with GitHub, along with its mature support for contributor workflows: anybody who wants to contribute can validate that their pull request is correct with the same processes we use ourselves. Unfortunately, there's a flipside to this: GitHub Actions has poor security defaults, and security compromises like those of Ultralytics, tj-actions, and Nx all began with well-trodden weaknesses like pwn requests. Here are some of the things we do to secure our CI/CD processes:
  4. We forbid many of GitHub's most dangerous and insecure triggers, such as pull_request_target and workflow_run, across our entire GitHub organization. These triggers are almost impossible to use securely and attackers keep finding ways to abuse them, so we simply don't allow them. Our experience with these triggers is that many projects think that they need them, but the overwhelming majority of their usages are better off being replaced with a less privileged trigger (such as pull_request) or removed entirely. For example, many projects use pull_request_target so that third-party contributor-triggered workflows can leave comments on PRs, but these use cases are often well served by job summaries or even just leaving the relevant information in the workflow's logs. Of course, there are some use cases that do require these triggers, such as anything that does really need to leave comments on third-party issues or pull requests. In these instances we recommend leaving GitHub Actions entirely and using a GitHub App (or webhook) that listens for the relevant events and acts in an independent context. We cover this pattern in more detail under Automations below.
  5. We require all actions to be pinned to specific commits (rather than tags or branches, which are mutable). Additionally, we cross-check these commits to ensure they match an actual released repository state and are not impostor commits. We do this in two ways: first with zizmor's unpinned-uses and impostor-commit audits, and again with GitHub's own "require actions to be pinned to a full-length commit SHA" policy. The former gives us a quick check that we can run locally (and prevents impostor commits), while the latter is a hard gate on workflow execution that actually ensures that all actions, including nested actions, are fully hash-pinned. Enabling the latter is a nontrivial endeavor, since it requires indirect action usages (the actions called by the actions we call) to be hash-pinned as well. To achieve this, we coordinated with our downstreams (example) to land hash-pinning across our entire dependency graph. Together, these checks increase our confidence in the reproducibility and hermeticity of our workflows, which in turn increases our confidence in their security (in the presence of an attacker's ability to compromise a dependent action). However, while necessary, this isn't sufficient: hash-pinning ensures that the action's contents are immutable, but doesn't prevent those immutable contents from making mutable decisions (such as installing the latest version of a binary from a GitHub repository's releases). Neither GitHub nor third-party tools perform well at detecting these kinds of immutability gaps yet, so we currently rely on manual review of our action dependencies to detect this class of risks. When manual review does identify gaps, we work with our upstreams to close them. For example, for actions that use native binaries internally, this is achieved by embedding a mapping between the download URL for the binary and a cryptographic hash. This hash in turn becomes part of the action's immutable state. While this doesn't ensure that the binary itself is authentic, it does ensure that an attacker cannot effectively tamper with a mutable pointer to the binary (such as a non-immutable tag or release).

12_throw_away 2026/04/09 17:04
我对 GitHub 的 CI 产品没有太多经验。但如果这是您安全使用它所需采取的步骤的准确描述……那么我不认为它 能事实上，永远要安全地使用。

即使你信任微软的后端云工程，这似乎也是一个连最基本的特权和隔离原则都不遵循的系统？我不确定你为什么会 尝试 在此基础上构建“供应链安全”。

I don't have much experience with GitHub's CI offering. But if this is an accurate description of the steps you need to take to use it securely ... then I don't think it can, in fact, ever be used securely.

Even if you trust Microsoft's cloud engineering on the backend, this is a system that does not appear to follow even the most basic principles of privilege and isolation? I'm not sure why you would even try to build "supply-chain security" on top of this.

hardsnow 2026/04/09 23:15
我同意这一点。我最近尝试弄清楚如何在 GitHub Actions 中正确保护代理编写的代码。我相信我成功地做到了这一点[1]，但安全配置最终变得如此微妙，以至于我对这是一条可扩展的路径不抱太大希望。

现在，正如其他评论者指出的那样，也许这只是这个领域固有的复杂性。但更安全的默认设置可能会大有帮助，使其在实践中更加安全。

[1] https://github.com/airutorg/sandbox-action

I would agree with this. I recently tried to figure out how to properly secure agent-authored code in GitHub Actions. I believe I succeeded in doing this[1] but the secure configuration ended up being so delicate that I don’t have high hopes of this being a scalable path.

Now, as other commenter pointed out, maybe this is just inherent complexity in this space. But more secure defaults could go a long way making this more secure in practice.

[1] https://github.com/airutorg/sandbox-action

在 HN 中查看
everforward 2026/04/09 22:20
我认为任何基于 Webhook 的提供商都更好，因为您可以隔离您的秘密。 PR 转到 PR Webhook，该 Webhook 在无法访问任何机密的环境中运行。

发布将转到发布 Webhook，该 Webhook 不应输出任何内容，理想情况下应该是具有防火墙规则和 DNS 块的单独计算机/VM，以防止流量流向非严格要求的任何地方。

然而，使用现代动态基础设施来确保安全要困难得多。让我感觉自己老了，但是当你可以说服务 X 有 IP Y 并在其周围添加防火墙规则时，事情就更简单了。如今，该服务可能有 15 个 IP 地址，并且每周更改一次。

I think any of the webhook-based providers are better, because you can isolate your secrets. PRs go to a PR webhook that runs in an environment that just doesn’t have access to any secrets.

Releases go to the release webhook, which should output nothing and ideally should be a separate machine/VM with firewall rules and DNS blocks that prevent traffic to anywhere not strictly required.

Things are a lot harder to secure with modern dynamic infrastructure, though. Makes me feel old, but things were simpler when you could say service X has IP Y and add firewall rules around it. Nowadays that service probably has 15 IP addresses that change once a week.

在 HN 中查看
WhyNotHugo 2026/04/10 00:24
复杂性来自于整个系统的设计方式。

没有任何发行版中常见的单一存储库或精选包：相反，操作会拉动其他操作，并且它们基本上是非常复杂的脚本包装器，可以从各处下载二进制文件。

对于许多非常简单的操作，不是安装分发包并运行单个命令，而是使用整个“操作”来创建该命令的整个抽象层。

这都是巨大的抽象之上的巨大复杂性，其中没有一个在设计时考虑到安全性：多年来它只是逐渐固定在上面。

The complexity comes from how the whole system is designed.

There’s no single repository or curated packages as is typical in any distribution: instead actions pull other actions, and they’re basically very complex wrapper around scripts which downloads binaries from all over the place.

For lots of very simple actions, instead of installing a distribution package and running a single command, a whole “action” is used which creates and entire layer of abstraction over that command.

It’s all massive complexity on top of huge abstractions, none of which were designed with security in mind: it was just gradually bolted on top over the years.

在 HN 中查看
lrvick 2026/04/10 04:40
如果您有确定性的完整源引导构建，那么它会变得非常简单，并且所有第三方信任都可以消失。

你构建它，我构建它，我们得到相同的哈希值。它允许任何人证明发布的二进制文件是给定输入源代码的忠实编译。

If you have deterministic full source bootstrapped builds, it gets pretty simple and all of the third party trust can go away.

You build it, I build it, we get the same hash. It allows anyone to prove a published binary is a faithful compilation of given input source code.

在 HN 中查看
12_throw_away 2026/04/10 03:36
是的，这个问题空间具有固有的复杂性，但是，这种固有的复杂性并不需要 Github 极其不安全的默认设置和不连贯的安全模型。

作为一个实际步骤，人们可以尝试使用 webhook 将他们的 github 存储库与任何其他 CI 提供商集成。这至少会给您一个单一的、低耦合的原语来构建您的工作流程。它不会以任何方式消除该领域固有的复杂性（秘密、第三方贡献、可信发布等），但它 开始安全 因为默认情况下它不会执行任何操作 - 它只是在某些条件下触发的 HTTP 调用。

Yes, this problem space has inherent complexity, but no, this inherent complexity does not require Github's insanely insecure defaults and incoherent security model.

As a practical step, one could try using webhooks to integrate their github repo with literally any other CI provider. This would at least give you a single, low-coupling primitive to build your workflows on. It would not, in any way, eliminate the domain's inherent complexity (secrets, 3rd party contributions, trusted publishing, etc.), but it starts out safe because by default it doesn't do anything - it's just an HTTP call that gets fired under certain conditions.

在 HN 中查看
pojzon 2026/04/10 04:27
这与任何软件包注册表导致某些软件包受到损害没有什么不同。

其中没有多少允许不可变的发布。如果他们这样做了，没有什么可以阻止您发布补丁版本，该版本很可能在构建过程中被许多项目自动引入。

整个依赖生态系统目前已被破坏。这就是为什么它如此容易（相对）通过供应链进行攻击。

真正安全的唯一方法是拥有自己的经过审查的依赖项注册表，固定到确切的版本并维护自己的升级管道。

没有人（除了谷歌）会这样做。成本太高了，光是这一块就需要两个大团队才能完成。

This is no different from any package registry getting some packages compromised.

Not many of them allow for immutable relases. And if they do, nothing blocks you from releasing a patch version that will most likely be automatically pulled in by many many projects during build.

The whole dependencies ecosystem is currently broken. Thats why its so easy (relatively) to attack via supply-chain.

Only way to be really secured is to have own registry of vetted dependencies pinned to exact version and maintain own upgrade pipeline.

NOONE (beside google) is going to do that. Its too costly, you need two big teams just to handle that one part.

在 HN 中查看
lrvick 2026/04/10 04:39
> 没有人（除了谷歌）会这样做。成本太高了，光是这一块就需要两个大团队才能完成。

然而，我和 stagex 的团队正在构建一个去中心化的代码审查系统来处理这个问题。不要等待企业为我们解决供应链安全问题。必须是社区主导的努力。

> NOONE (beside google) is going to do that. Its too costly, you need two big teams just to handle that one part.

And yet my team and I at stagex are building a decentralized code review system to handle this anyway. Not waiting around with our fingers crossed for the corpos to solve supply chain security for us. Has to be a community led effort.

lrvick 2026/04/09 16:16
世界上您可以获得的唯一从签名包提交到签名评论再到多签名确定性工件的完整源代码引导的 uv 二进制文件是我和我在 stagex 的队友的二进制文件。

维护人员持有的地理分布式智能卡上的所有密钥都与信任网络相关联，该网络可追溯到 25 年前，拥有超过 5000 个密钥。

https://stagex.tools/packages/core/uv/

尽管感谢客户偶尔让个人维护人员在 stagex 上兼职工作，但我们还是收到过一笔 50 美元的项目捐赠。 （谢谢）

为什么一群大多无偿的志愿者黑客在供应链安全方面投入的精力比 OpenAI 更多。

我很生气。

The only binaries of uv in the world you can get that were full source bootstrapped from signed package commits to signed reviews to multi-signed deterministic artifacts are the ones from my teammates and I at stagex.

All keys on geodistributed smartcards held by maintainers tied to a web of trust going back 25 years with over 5000 keys.

https://stagex.tools/packages/core/uv/

Though thankful for clients that let individual maintainers work on stagex part time once in a while, we have had one donation ever for $50 as a project. (thanks)

Why is it a bunch of mostly unpaid volunteer hackers are putting more effort into supply chain security than OpenAI.

I am annoyed.

jmalicki 2026/04/09 23:28
这是市场告诉你什么是重要的。

OpenClaw 取得了巨大的成功，它为人们提供了泄露密钥、秘密和个人数据的能力，并允许人们在用户感觉自己的攻击面太低时遭受数量惊人的供应链攻击。

当市场强烈向您发出信号，表明人们希望降低安全性和增加供应链攻击时，您一直在努力提高安全性和减少供应链攻击！

This is the market telling you what matters.

OpenClaw has been an outstanding success, it is providing people the ability to leak their keys, secrets, and personal data, and allowing people to be subject to an incredible number of supply chain attacks when its users have felt their attack surface was just too low.

Your efforts have been on increasing security and reducing supply chain attacks, when the market is strongly signaling to you that people want reduced security and more supply chain attacks!

woodruffw 2026/04/09 21:36
（我是《TFA》的作者。）

> 维护人员持有的地理分布式智能卡上的所有密钥都与信任网络相关，该网络可追溯到 25 年前，拥有超过 5000 个密钥。

如果我不信任维护者本身，那么关键图的年龄和基数都不会告诉我任何信息；鉴于您从根本上提供第三方构建，您要解决的威胁模型是什么？

值得一提的是，所有 uv 的构建都来自锁定的分辨率，并且正如 TFA 中提到的，您可以从我们这里获得签名的工件。所以我对来自 a 的签名包提交的价值非常模糊。 不同的 一组身份，而不是实际构建软件的身份。

(I’m the author of TFA.)

> All keys on geodistributed smartcards held by maintainers tied to a web of trust going back 25 years with over 5000 keys.

Neither the age nor the cardinality of the key graph tells me anything if I don’t trust the maintainers themselves; given that you’re fundamentally providing third-party builds, what’s the threat model you’re addressing?

It’s worth nothing that all builds of uv come from a locked resolution and, as mentioned in TFA, you can get signed artifacts from us. So I’m very murky on the value of signed package commits that come from a different set of identities than the ones actually building the software.

在 HN 中查看
kaathewise 2026/04/09 22:11
StageX 进行可重复的构建，因此它们是独立签名的，也可以在本地进行验证。 我认为它不适用于 Astral，但它对于只有一个维护者或易受攻击的 CI 的软件包很有用，因为这些软件包只有一个故障点。

但我也认为，如果项目提供第一方 StageX 构建，就像许多项目使用 Dockerfile 或 Nix flake 所做的那样，那就太好了。

StageX does reproducible builds, so they are signed independently and can also be verified locally. I don't think it applies to Astral, but it's useful for packages with a single maintainer or a vulnerable CI, where there is only one point of failure.

But I also think it'd be nice if projects provided a first-party StageX build, like many do with a Dockerfile or a Nix flake.

lrvick 2026/04/10 03:22
您绝对信任当前 CI 解决方案的每一层中已经存在的相同的信任密钥图网络。 Astral 的一切以及所有迹象表明 OpenAI 都是通过第三方服务、第三方（盲）签名构建的，使用由这 5000 个密钥直接或间接签名的第三方二进制文件。

该信任网络是整个互联网以及可能为 Github、Astral 和 OpenAI 提供支持的每台服务器（包括您描述的每个 CI 系统）的信任基础。

https://kron.fi/en/posts/stagex-web-of-trust/

该图中的一个节点也远不足以阻止供应链攻击，这就是为什么我们使用“多个”点，这要归功于完全源引导的确定性构建。

让我反问一下，为什么任何人都应该相信 Astral/OpenAI 员工不签署他们的提交，不签署他们的评论，没有被假冒或由于允许的网络钓鱼 2FA 而被接管，并且不会只以笔名向 uv（或 uv 本身！）的 CI 堆栈进行提交，然后合并他们的笔名的代码。

尽管采用了这篇博文中的做法，一个人仍可以将其全部烧毁。让机器盲目地签署自动化过程中产生的任何非确定性输出实际上并不会为您在实践中对抗实际使用的许多供应链攻击策略带来多大好处。当然，这同样适用于您信任的第三方构建系统。 Github 本身也没有使用任何这些基本的供应链安全实践，也没有使用太多的故障点。

Astral/OpenAI 实际上给了除了作者之外的数千个随机数，能够对您生成的 uv 二进制文件进行后门处理，并且如果没有可重复的完整源引导构建过程，没有人能够快速或轻松地证明这一点。

要在 stagex 中打包或更改 uv，一名维护者必须签署提交，另一名维护者必须签署审查/合并提交。然后，多个维护者必须编译 180 字节的人类可读机器代码，构建为tinycc，然后是 gcc，然后是 llvm，最后是 rust 编译器，然后我们用它来构建 uv，所有这些都是确定性的。

因此，在我们的流程中，我们实际上在有限程度上不信任除源代码的实际作者之外的任何第三方。也就是说，我们现在也在研究一种对上游代码进行去中心化审查的解决方案，因为我们很大程度上不相信上游不会让他们的身份被盗，因为大多数团队出于某种原因拒绝签署他们的提交和审查，所以我们也必须为他们这样做。无论如何，我们可以证明我们忠实地交付了发布的任何上游代码的诚实编译，没有任何单点故障。

我们要求下载二进制文件的用户相信，一群维护者正在将他们的个人声誉和密钥（早在人工智能出现之前就已经存在，并且很难模仿）在线上签署他们的完全相同的 uv 版本及其下面的整个工具链，并提供上游源代码的忠实编译。

如果上游，尤其是资金充足的上游，能够达到或超过我们必须支持下游的威胁模型，这将使​​每个人都更加安全。

You definitely trust the same web of trust key graph already in every single layer of your current CI solution. Everything at Astral and by all indications also OpenAI is built with third party services, third party (blind) signing, using third party binaries signed by those 5000 keys directly or indirectly.

That web of trust is the trust foundation of the entire internet and likely every server that powers Github, Astral, and OpenAI including every CI system you described.

https://kron.fi/en/posts/stagex-web-of-trust/

One node in that graph is also nowhere near good enough to stop supply chain attacks, which is why we use -multiple- points thanks to full source bootstrapped deterministic builds.

Let me flip it and ask why anyone should trust that an Astral/OpenAI employee that does not sign their commits and does not sign their reviews, has not been impersonated or had an account takeover due to the phishable 2FA that is allowed, and won't just make a commit to CI stack for uv (or uv itself!) under a pseudonym then merge their pseudonym's code.

One person can burn it all down in spite of the practices in this blog post. Letting machines blindly sign whatever non-deterministic outputs come out of an automated process does not actually buy you much in practice against many of the supply chain attack tactics actually used in the wild. Also of course the same applies to the third party build systems you trust. Github themselves also don't use any of these basic supply chain security practices either so many many points of failure here.

Astral/OpenAI are actually giving -thousands- of randos other than the authors the ability to backdoor the uv binaries you produce, and without a reproducible full source bootstrapped build process, no one would be able to quickly or easily prove it.

To package or change uv in stagex one maintainer must sign the commit, and another must sign the review/merge commit. Then -multiple- maintainers must compile 180 bytes of human readable machine code, build up to tinycc, then gcc, then llvm, and eventually to a rust compiler, that we then use to build uv, all deterministically.

So, we actually don't trust any third parties other than the actual authors of the source code to a limited extent in our process. That said we are working on a solution for decentralized review of upstream code as well right now because we largely don't trust upstreams to not let their identities get stolen because most teams for whatever reason refuse to sign their commits and reviews, so we will have to do that for them too. Regardless, we can prove we faithfully deliver honest compilations of whatever upstream code is published without any single points of failure.

We ask users downloading binaries to trust that a bunch of maintainers are putting their personal reputations and keys (which long predate AI and are hard to impersonate) on the line to sign their bit for bit identical builds of uv, and the entire toolchain underneath it, and provide faithful compilations of upstream source code.

It would make everyone a lot safer if upstreams, especially well funded ones, could meet or exceed the threat model we must support downstream.

在 HN 中查看
woodruffw 2026/04/10 03:34
> 您绝对信任当前 CI 解决方案的每一层中已有的相同信任密钥图网络。 Astral 的一切以及所有迹象表明 OpenAI 都是通过第三方服务、第三方（盲）签名构建的，使用由这 5000 个密钥直接或间接签名的第三方二进制文件。

我不认为我们这样做；在某些地方我们信任分发签名者，但我们不会在“网络”拓扑中这样做；我们之所以信任它们，是因为一小组密钥被预先烘焙到虚拟机、Docker 镜像等中。20 年前存在的信任网络已经消亡[1]。

从拓扑上来说，这很像一个 CA 生态系统，除了物质方面更糟糕：即使是发行版（充满了才华横溢、积极进取的人！）也很难实施 PGP，所以我们最终得到了一堆 实际上 没有人真正跟踪的不可过期且不可撤销的密钥[2]。因此，没有人真正将这些因素纳入他们的安全故事中，无论它们是否是网络。

[1]： https://inversegravity.net/2019/web-of-trust-dead/

[2]： https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/apt/+bug/1461834

> You definitely trust the same web of trust key graph already in every single layer of your current CI solution. Everything at Astral and by all indications also OpenAI is built with third party services, third party (blind) signing, using third party binaries signed by those 5000 keys directly or indirectly.

I don't think we do; there are places we trust distribution signers, but we don't do so in a "web" topology; we trust them because a small set of keys is pre-baked into VMs, Docker images, etc. The web of trust, as it existed 20 years ago, is dead[1].

Topologically this is a lot like a CA ecosystem, except worse in material ways: even distros (full of talented, motivated people!) struggle to operationalize PGP, so we end up with a bunch of de facto unexpirable and irrevocable keys[2] that nobody is really tracking. Consequently, nobody is really factoring these into their security story, whether or not they're a web.

[1]: https://inversegravity.net/2019/web-of-trust-dead/

[2]: https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/apt/+bug/1461834

在 HN 中查看
lrvick 2026/04/10 04:14
你可以说它已经死了，然而，由于 Debian、Suse、Fedora、Arch、Gentoo、Ubuntu、Redhat 等，它是目前唯一大规模签名互联网基础设施的系统。对于那些最需要它的用例来说，它非常活跃。如果这些密钥之一或持有密钥的人被泄露，则大多数互联网都会被后门保护。除了基于 stagex 构建的东西，在这种情况下你将不得不妥协一些。仍然不够好，但更好了。

PGP 信任网络尽管有其所有缺陷和早期设计的面部识别（其中有很多），但它是我们在人工智能之前构建的唯一人类证明系统，在该系统中，人类与人类相遇并互相签署密钥。没有人可以合理地期望任何最近创建的密钥不是由编造的 LLM 身份创建的，除非通过众所周知且值得信赖的人持有的公开发布的现有密钥登录到信任网络。

但即使您不想查看 Web of Trust，您也可以通过各种其他方式证明我签署 stagex 版本的密钥是我的，这要归功于 keyoxy： https://keyoxide.org/E90A401336C8AAA9

此外，PGP 规范现在支持现代加密、通过 dns 的证明，甚至黑客新闻。您可以证明我的 PGP 密钥现在已与我的 HN 个人资料绑定。我同意 -gpg- 已经死了，既然我们有了带有现代加密技术的现代 Rust 工具，就没有真正的理由再使用它了。

但！如果有人想在智能卡或其他东西上生成 ssh 密钥并用它进行签名，我们绝对会考虑它。不只支持单一规范，但我们绝对需要人类在智能卡上持有自己的私钥，这些私钥本身由其他人类持有的私钥和相同身份共享的在线服务证明。

You can call it dead, and yet, it is the only system signing internet infrastructure at scale right now because of Debian, Suse, Fedora, Arch, Gentoo, Ubuntu, Redhat, etc. It is very much alive for those use cases that need it most. If one of those keys was compromised or the people that hold them, most of the internet is backdoored. Except for things built on stagex, in which case you would have to compromise several. Still not good enough, but better.

PGP Web of Trust for all its faults and early design facepalms (of which there are many) is the only proof-of-human system where humans meet humans and sign each others keys that we ever built before AI. No one can reasonably expect any recently created keys were not created by made up LLM identities unless signed into the web of trust by well published existing keys held by well known and trusted humans.

But even if you don't want to look at the Web of Trust you can prove the key I sign stagex releases with is mine via all sorts of other ways thanks to keyoxide: https://keyoxide.org/E90A401336C8AAA9

Also PGP specs supports modern crypto now, attestation via dns, and even hackernews. You can attest my PGP key is tied to my HN profile right now. I would agree -gpg- is dead, with no real reason to use it anymore now that we have modern rust tooling with modern crypto.

But! If someone wants to generate an ssh key on a smartcard or something and sign with that instead, we would absolutely consider it. Not married to supporting only a single spec, but we absolutely need human beings to hold their own private keys on smartcards which are themselves attested by other human held private keys and the online services shared by the same identities.

在 HN 中查看
woodruffw 2026/04/10 04:35
不，我称它为“死”，因为它已经死了。 SKS 网络已经死亡，强大的集合也已垂死，而 PGP 的剩余真实用户则通过将密钥包烘焙成预先信任的工件（如 ISO）来散布密钥包。但这不是一个“信任网”，它只是定制的集中式密钥分发，其认证格式是每个严肃的密码学家都认为很糟糕的。

（这是在一个更残酷的统计论证之前：即使在最大程度上，PGP 生态系统也 微小的[1]。）

[1]： https://moxie.org/2015/02/24/gpg-and-me.html

No, I call it dead because it's dead. The SKS network is dead, the strong set is moribund, and the remaining real users of PGP are instead slinging key bundles around by baking them into pre-trusted artifacts (like ISOs). But that's not a "web of trust," it's just bespoke centralized key distribution with a certification format that every single serious cryptographer agrees is terrible.

(And this is before a more brute statistical argument: even at its greatest extent, the PGP ecosystem was minuscule[1].)

[1]: https://moxie.org/2015/02/24/gpg-and-me.html

在 HN 中查看
lrvick 2026/04/10 04:44
我深知莫克西对此的看法，我们也详细讨论过，他是错的。而且 SKS 和 GnuPG 也不是 OpenPGP。 GnuPG 不再符合现代 OpenPGP 标准，并且是 IE6 级实现，我们应该停止谈论和使用，在这一点上至少 moxie 和我同意。我自己在 gpg 中发现了一个主要的 CVE。

但不管工具如何，关键在于钥匙、谁拥有它们以及它们支持谁。密钥如何分发并不重要。重要的是，密钥签署了其他密钥，并且我们有办法下载它们并验证它们。

我们在我们的密钥存储库中的 stagex 维护者信任网络中缓存了所有 5444 个密钥的副本，您可以从我们的密钥到今天签署的 Linux 内核提交的密钥之间划出一条线。这些还从十几个 SKS 密钥服务器进行同步和更新，这些服务器仍然在线，供任何想要像我们一样构建密钥目录的人使用。

尽管 SKS 正在迅速被 WKD 取代，其中每个域都托管自己的密钥，并且它们会被自动发现。

您真的要说这没有信任或安全价值吗？

我们都应该停下来，让 Github 为我们签署所有内容，即使他们没有完整的源引导任何内容或签署提交或使用确定性构建？

您在这里实际上争论的结果是什么？

I am deeply aware of Moxies views on this, and we have talked about them at length, and he is wrong. Also SKS and GnuPG are not OpenPGP. GnuPG no longer conforms to modern OpenPGP standards and is the IE6 grade implementation that we should stop talking about and using and on that point at least moxie and I agree. I found a major CVE in gpg myself.

But regardless of tooling, it is about the keys and who holds them and who they endorse. It does not really matter how keys are distributed. It matters that keys signed other keys and that we have a way of downloading them and verifying that.

We cache a copy of all 5444 keys in the web of trust of stagex maintainers in our keys repo and you can draw a line from our keys to the keys that signed commits to the linux kernel today. These also sync and update from a dozen SKS keyservers that are still online for anyone that wants to build a key directory as we did.

Though SKS is being rapidly replaced with WKD where every domain hosts their own keys and they are automatically discovered.

Are you really going to say this has no trust or security value?

We should all just stop and let Github sign everything for us even though they don't full source bootstrap anything or sign commits or use deterministic builds?

What is the outcome you are actually arguing for here.

在 HN 中查看
woodruffw 2026/04/10 05:22
> 密钥如何分配并不重要。重要的是，密钥签署了其他密钥，并且我们有办法下载它们并验证它们。

我认为如果你想将其称为 WoT，这一点很重要。而且，我认为源自这些密钥的任何签名都没有得到验证 有用地 在任何有意义的规模。

> 您真的要说这没有信任或安全价值吗？

我认为它具有边际安全价值，如果你将它与密码学家和密码工程师不得不浪费时间争论反对使用 PGP 的事实相平衡，也许是净负值。

> 您在这里实际上争论的结果是什么？

我喜欢二进制透明度。我还认为基于身份的签名明显更符合人体工程学，并且在过去 4 年中比 PGP 在过去 35 年中得到更多采用。我认为这实际上是一个令人震惊的控诉，因为我想说像 Sigstore 这样的基于身份的签名方案 仍然 落后于我的期望。

> It does not really matter how keys are distributed. It matters that keys signed other keys and that we have a way of downloading them and verifying that.

I think it matters if you want to call it a WoT. But also, I don't think any signatures originating from these keys are being verified usefully at any meaningful scale.

> Are you really going to say this has no trust or security value?

I think it has marginal security value, maybe net-negative if you balance it with the fact that cryptographers and cryptographic engineers have to waste time arguing against using PGP.

> What is the outcome you are actually arguing for here.

I like binary transparency. I also think identity-based signing is significantly more ergonomic, and has seen more adoption in the last 4 years than PGP has in the last 35. And I think this is actually a stunning indictment, because I'd say that identity-based signing schemes like Sigstore are still running behind my expectations.

在 HN 中查看
lrvick 2026/04/10 05:44
> 我认为如果你想将其称为 WoT，这一点很重要。而且，我认为源自这些密钥的任何签名都没有在任何有意义的规模上得到有效验证。

信任网络是相互信任密钥的网络，而不是服务器网络。该网络可以在任何计算机上进行验证，如我之前链接的 kron 的博客文章中所示，并且我们即将发布的 sxctl 工具中的每个软件包安装都会经过验证，我们将在下个月的一些会议上展示。

> 我认为它具有边际安全价值，如果您将其与密码学家和密码工程师不得不浪费时间争论反对使用 PGP 的事实相平衡，则可能是净负值。

那么，您真的是说大多数运行互联网的服务的所有维护者都应该停止使用唯一的 IETF 标准，该标准是为使用这些人持有的密钥进行人类身份绑定签名而构建的吗？

每个人似乎都板着脸建议的替代方案是使用 github 或 google 登录，然后让他们用“无密钥签名”为你签名？这是唯一获得采用的替代方案，而且这是一个荒谬的降级。我认为主要是安全剧院。

人们在本地持有自己的签名密钥的全部目的是能够使您的集中式在线帐户被接管时变得无关紧要。这通常很容易做到，因为没有人使用硬件 2FA 或续订其个人电子邮件域。

但是，如果他们确实使用了硬件 2FA，嘿，看看他们有一个本地签名密钥...为什么不直接...用该硬件签署二进制文件，而不是使用它来登录并让其他人为您签名。然后，如果您打算这样做，您不想被冒充，那么为什么不发布这些公钥，并让其他维护者签署它们。现在我们重新发明了信任网络。

> I think it matters if you want to call it a WoT. But also, I don't think any signatures originating from these keys are being verified usefully at any meaningful scale.

Web of trust is a web of mutually trusting keys, not a network of servers. That web can be verified on any computer as in the blog post by kron I linked earlier, and it is verified for every package install in our soon-to-be published sxctl tool we will be presenting at some conferences next month.

> I think it has marginal security value, maybe net-negative if you balance it with the fact that cryptographers and cryptographic engineers have to waste time arguing against using PGP.

So again, are you really saying all the maintainers of most services running the internet should stop using the only IETF standard built for human-identity-bound signing with keys held by those humans?

The alternative everyone seems to be suggesting with a straight face is login with github or google and let them sign for you with "keyless signing"? That is the only alternative that is gaining adoption, and it is a ridiculous downgrade. I consider it mostly security theater.

The whole point of humans holding their own signing keys locally is to be able to make it not matter if your centralized online accounts are taken over. Something that is usually easy to do because no one uses hardware 2FA or renews their personal email domains.

But, if they did use hardware 2FA, hey look they have a local signing key... why not just... sign the binaries with that hardware directly instead of using that to login and let someone else sign for you. And then if you are going to do that, you don't want to be impersonated, so why not publish those public keys, and have other maintainers sign them. And now we have re-invented the web of trust.

我不认为你生气了。您这样做是为了生成一个可复制的 Linux 发行版，您的合作伙伴将为其提供支持。

我一点也不觉得这很烦人——我预计必须对数百个包执行此操作。

没有无偿志愿者，像 Debian 这样的东西就不会存在。不要诽谤其他项目的情况和环境，尤其是当他们是你的竞争对手时。

通过变得更好来竞争，而不是通过大声抱怨来竞争。

I don't think you are annoyed. You have done this to produce a reproducible linux distribution which your partners sell support for.

I wouldn't find this annoying at all - I would expect to have to do this for hundreds of packages.

Without unpaid volunteers things like Debian do not exist. Don't malign the situation and circumstances of other projects, especially if they are your competitors.

Compete by being better, not by complaining louder.

lrvick 2026/04/10 03:40
当然，个人维护者会提供通用咨询服务，但如果我们在接下来的 20 年里都这样做以保持正常运转，那么我们赚到的钱将永远不会像 Chainguard 和其他人那样通过付费二进制工件赚到的钱的一小部分。

Stagex is and will forever be a community owned project.

OpenAI wants to make the most money. Why would they spend any time or money on something they can get for free?

我可能很愚蠢，但 AGPLv3 并没有阻止 $Evilcorp 使用它，他们只需要分享他们所做的任何修改或分叉？
I’m maybe daft but AGPLv3 doesnt prevent $Evilcorp from using it, they just need to share any modifications or forks they made?
在 HN 中查看
skrtskrt 2026/04/10 08:25
事实上，大多数 $Evilcorp 都有针对 AGPLv3 的政策，这就是为什么项目可以让 moneh 出售相同代码的限制较少的企业许可证。
In reality most $Evilcorp have policies against AGPLv3, which is why projects can make moneh selling a less-restricted enterprise license for the same code.
在 HN 中查看
MidnightRider39 2026/04/10 09:12
我经常听到这句话，但我并不真正理解。并不是说您需要向我解释，但是 AGPLv3 的问题是什么导致这些公司望而却步？

在我这个非律师眼中，它看起来像 MIT 或 Apache2，但修改也需要公开。

如果不做任何修改应该没问题吧？ 或者大多数 $Evilcorp 的目标是进行修改？ 还是 AGPLv3 就像大蒜对抗吸血鬼一样（没有意义，但似乎有效）？

I often hear this but I don’t really understand it. Not saying you need to explain it to me but what is the issue with AGPLv3 that turns those corporations away?

To my non-lawyer eyes it looks like MIT or Apache2 but modifications need to be made public as well.

If you don’t make any modifications then it should be fine? Or do most $Evilcorp aim to make modifications? Or is AGPLv3 something like garlic against vampires (doesn’t make sense but seems to work)?

在 HN 中查看
skrtskrt 2026/04/10 09:36
AGPLv3 规定，“分发”实质上包括通过网络与服务进行通信，这与 GPL 概念相反，即发送收缩包装的二进制文件，供某人下载并运行。

因此，基本上他们担心，他们无法避免数万名工程师中的一名或多名工程师通过将其包含在某种可公开访问的服务中来将其“分发”给客户。 AFAIK 关于什么级别的网络通信符合资格还没有确定的案例 - 例如，如果我在 Postgres 上运行 CRUD 应用程序，而 Postgres 是 AGPL，我是否分发 Postgres？

现在第二部分是你只需要给出你的 变化 到 AGPL 软件到它“分发”到的那些人。大多数人都没有改变它！如果有什么的话，他们只是在它前面运行一个控制平面......

但这又回到了“安全总比后悔好”的公司法律视角——我们不能保证我们的一位工程师不会以某种会暴露公司内部结构的方式对其进行更改，然后触发他们必须公开分发这些私人更改的情况。

AGPLv3 includes that “distribution” includes essentially communicating with the service over the network, as opposed to the GPL concept of like, sending a shrink wrapped binary that someone downloads and runs themselves.

So basically they are worried that they have no way of avoiding one or more of their tens of thousands of engineers “distributing” it to customers by including it in some sort of publicly accessible service. AFAIK there’s no settled case regarding what level of network communication qualifies - like if I run a CRUD app on Postgres and Postgres was AGPL, am I distributing Postgres?

Now the second part is that you only have to give out your changes to the AGPL software to those that it was “distributed” to. Most people aren’t changing it! If anything they’re just running a control plane in front of it…

but it goes back to the corporate legal perspective of “better safe than sorry” - we can’t guarantee that one of our engineers, isn’t changing it in some way that would expose company internals, then triggering a condition where they have to distribute those private changes publicly.

在 HN 中查看
trollbridge 2026/04/10 02:16
在这一点上，通过法学硕士运行代码来翻译它似乎消除了版权（以及许可证），因此 $Anycorp 可以使用它。

我们的产品已获得 AGPL3 许可，如果目前的趋势继续下去，我们可能会转向 MIT，这样至少小公司可以像大公司一样利用它。

And at this point, it appears running code through an LLM to translate it eliminates copyright (and thus the licence), so $Anycorp can use it.

Our stuff is AGPL3 licenced and if this present trend continues we might just switch to MIT so at least the little guys can take advantage of it the way the big guys can.

I think the whitewashing of code through LLMs is still unproven if it actually works for a reasonably complex project and also it’s still kind of legal Wild West - I think no one knows for sure how it will work out.

Only if they provide the software or software as a service. Then I suspect it's good enough if the modifications or forks made are shared internally if software is used only internally, but on the other hand I'm not a lawyer.

fweimer 2026/04/10 01:19
雇主可能会争辩说，此类内部使用和分发属于 GPLv3 中“完全代表您”的条款，该条款由 AGPLv3 继承。
Employers might argue that such internal use and distribution would fall under the “exclusively under your behalf” clause in the GPLv3, which is inherited by the AGPLv3.

fsflover 2026/04/10 00:02
这就是重点。他们可以使用和修改它，但他们也必须分享他们的修改，即帮助它的发展。然而大多数大型企业甚至从未接触过这个许可证。
This is the point. They can use and modify it, but they also have to share their modifications, i.e., help its development. Yet most megacorps never even touch this license.

tclancy 2026/04/09 19:46

Never let the left hand know what the right hand is doing. I suppose it works both ways here, but the specific end user is not why people make code available, it’s in the hope of improving things, even just the tiniest bit.

saghm 2026/04/09 18:27
> 为什么一群大多无偿的志愿者黑客在供应链安全方面投入的精力比 OpenAI 更多。

这次收购不是几周前才发生的吗？如果 OpenAI 介入并迫使他们改变构建过程，这不是更令人震惊吗？除非你声称这篇文章是在描述他们已经做了一段时间的事情（如果没有更多证据，这似乎有点奇怪），否则我不清楚为什么你将这个过程归因于母公司。

别误会我的意思；有 足够 你可以批评 OpenAI ，我不会对你的技术主张采取立场，但这样说似乎有点不诚实。

> Why is it a bunch of mostly unpaid volunteer hackers are putting more effort into supply chain security than OpenAI.

Didn't the acquisition only happen a few weeks ago? Wouldn't it be more alarming if OpenAI had gone in and forced them to change their build process? Unless you're claiming that the article is lying about this being a description of what they've already been doing for a while (which seems a bit outlandish without more evidence), it's not clear to me why you're attributing this process to the parent company.

Don't get me wrong; there's plenty you can criticize OpenAI over, and I'm not taking a stance on your technical claims, but it seems somewhat disingenuous to phrase it like this.

woodruffw 2026/04/09 22:44

Yeah, I'll just establish for the record that we've been thinking about this for a long time, and that it has nothing to do with anybody except our own interests in keeping our development and release processes secure.

在 HN 中查看
lrvick 2026/04/10 03:48
crev 的最大问题是（我上次检查过）它完全集中在 git/github 上，这使得它对于早期供应链依赖项没有那么有用，这些依赖项通常只存在于服务器、svn、cvs 或 Mercurial 上的随机 tar 文件上。 Crev 还缺乏对公共身份绑定密钥的支持，这将使我们放弃我们在身份绑定密钥中构建的高度有价值的 25 年以上信任网络，这些密钥早于人工智能并且无法轻易冒充。

此外，他们自己也不签署提交或评论，因为他们认为 crev 消除了对此类事情的需要，我认为这是荒谬的。

我真的很喜欢 dpc，并且在他设计 crev 时在他旁边工作，并试图解释这些确切的问题，但最终他想要发布的东西只能解决他当时关心的有限问题集，这就是祝福 github 上的 rust 包，他当然有权这样做。

我们仍然肯定会引用 crev，并且我们正在纳入一些我们认为好的想法，例如评论的实际总体形状、信心等。

The biggest problem with crev is it is (last I checked) entirely centralized on git/github making it not all that useful for early supply chain dependencies that often just live on random tar files on servers, or svn, or cvs, or mercurial. Crev also lacks support for public identity bound keys which would make us give up the highly valuable 25+ year web of trust we have built in identity bound keys that predate AI and cannot be easily impersonated.

Also they don't sign commits or reviews themselves because they think crev eliminates the need for such things, which I consider ridiculous.

I really like dpc and worked next to him when he was designing crev and tried to explain these exact problems, but in the end he wanted to ship something that only solved the limited set of problems he cared about at the time which was blessing rust packages on github, which he is of course entitled to do.

We will still certainly cite crev and we are incorporating some of what we feel are the good ideas such as the actual general shape of the reviews, confidence, etc.

charcircuit 2026/04/09 20:17
>为什么一群大多无偿的志愿者黑客在供应链安全方面投入的精力比 OpenAI 更多。

坦白说。因为更多的努力实际上并不意味着某些东西更安全。仅仅因为您检查了额外的内容或采取了额外的步骤，并不意味着它实际上会带来明显更好的安全性。

>Why is it a bunch of mostly unpaid volunteer hackers are putting more effort into supply chain security than OpenAI.

To be frank. Because more effort doesn't actually mean that something is more secure. Just because you check extra things or take extra steps that doesn't mean it actually results in tangibly better security.

dirkc 2026/04/09 15:06
开源生态系统已经取得了长足的进步，并被证明具有弹性。尽管信任仍将是任何生态系统的重要组成部分，但在沙盒第三方代码方面，我们迫切需要改进我们的工具和实践。

几乎每次我在项目工作中遇到 uv 时，它所吹捧的好处是它可以更轻松地运行具有不同 python 版本的项目，并避免第三个依赖项的冲突 - 基本上是 pyenv + venv + speed。

这让我脊背发凉，因为它告诉我人们在零沙箱的主机上运行所有这些不同的工具。

The open source ecosystem has come very far and proven to be resilient. And while trust will remain a crucial part of any ecosystem, we urgently need to improve our tools and practices when it comes to sandboxing 3rd party code.

Almost every time I bump into uv in project work, the touted benefit is that it makes it easier to run projects with different python versions and avoiding clashes of 3rd dependencies - basically pyenv + venv + speed.

That sends a cold shiver down my spine, because it tells me that people are running all these different tools on their host machine with zero sandboxing.

silvester23 2026/04/09 17:38
对于我们来说，uv 的 DX 用于依赖管理比仅仅使用 pip 和requirements.txt 好得多。

但要明确的是，我们仅在 docker 构建的构建器阶段使用 uv，最终图像中没有 uv。

For us, the DX of uv for dependency management is much better than just using pip and requirements.txt.

To be clear though, we only use uv in the builder stage of our docker builds, there is no uv in the final image.

在 HN 中查看
sersi 2026/04/09 15:35
我现在使用 uv 的主要原因是能够指定冷却时间。 pip 允许这样做，但它带有时间戳，所以几乎没用。

这并不妨碍我将其运行到沙箱或虚拟机中以获得额外的安全层。

Main reason I now use uv is being able to specify a cool down period. pip allows it but it's with a timestamp so pretty much useless..

And that doesn't prevent me from running it into a sandbox or vm for an additional layer of security.

在 HN 中查看
raphinou 2026/04/09 14:07
当前软件供应链的一个（除其他外）大问题是，下载了大量工具和依赖项（例如从 GitHub 版本），而没有验证其是否由预期作者发布。这就是为什么我致力于开发开源、可审计、无帐户、可自托管、多签名文件身份验证解决方案。多重签名方法可以防止类似 axios 的破坏。如果您对此感兴趣，请看一下 https://asfaload.com/
One (amongst other) big problem with current software supply chain is that a lot of tools and dependencies are downloaded (eg from GitHub releases) without any validation that it was published by the expected author. That's why I'm working on an open source, auditable, accountless, self hostable, multi sig file authentication solution. The multi sig approach can protect against axios-like breaches. If this is of interest to you, take a look at https://asfaload.com/

在 HN 中查看
raphinou 2026/04/09 14:32
是的，这就是为什么我的目标是让检查对用户透明。您只需提供下载网址即可进行身份验证。我真的需要录制一个小演示。
Yes, that's why I aim to make the checks transparant to the user. You only need to provide the download url for the authentication to take place. I really need to record a small demo of it.
在 HN 中查看
raphinou 2026/04/09 14:28
工件证明确实是另一种基于的解决方案 https://www.sigstore.dev/ 。我仍然认为 Asfaload 是一个不错的选择，它做出了与 sigstore 不同的选择：

- Asfaload 是无帐户的（密钥是身份），而 sigstore 依赖于 openid connect[1]，这会将大多数用户与大型企业联系起来

- Asfaload 的后端是一个公共 git，使其易于审核

- Asfaload 将很容易自行托管，这意味着您可以轻松地在内部部署它

- Asfaload 是多重签名的，这意味着如果 GitHub 帐户被破坏，就可以检测到恶意工件

- 验证下载对用户来说是透明的，只需要下载 url，这与 sigstore [2] 相反

因此，Asfaload 并不是唯一的解决方案，但我认为它有一些独特的特性，值得评估。

1：https://docs.sigstore.dev/about/security/

2： https://docs.sigstore.dev/cosign/verifying/verify/

Artifact attestation are indeed another solution based on https://www.sigstore.dev/ . I still think Asfaload is a good alternative, making different choices than sigstore:

- Asfaload is accountless(keys are identity) while sigstore relies on openid connect[1], which will tie most user to a mega corp

- Asfaload ' backend is a public git, making it easily auditable

- Asfaload will be easy to self host, meaning you can easily deploy it internally

- Asfaload is multisig, meaning event if GitHub account is breached, malevolent artifacts can be detected

- validating a download is transparant to the user, which only requires the download url, contrary to sigstore [2]

So Asfaload is not the only solution, but I think it has some unique characteristics that make it worth evaluating.

1:https://docs.sigstore.dev/about/security/

2: https://docs.sigstore.dev/cosign/verifying/verify/

Overall I believe this is the right approach and something like this is what's required. I can't see any code or your product though so I'm not sure what to make of it.

est 2026/04/09 16:29
> 没有任何验证它是由预期作者发布的

单点故障。我建议使用自动工具来审核每一行代码，无论作者是谁。

在 HN 中查看
s_ting765 2026/04/09 19:05
如果固定操作本身引入了其他本身可能受到损害的依赖项，则通过提交 SHA 固定 github 操作并不能解决供应链问题。例如，一个操作可以拉入 docker 映像作为依赖项。它实际上是一个安全剧院。真正的解决办法是拥有在 CI 管道中运行的代码。或者分叉操作本身并将其作为基础设施的一部分进行维护。
Pinning github actions by commit SHA does not solve the supply chain problem if the pinned action itself is pulling in other dependencies which themselves could be compromised. An action can pull in a docker image as a dependency for example. It is effectively security theatre. The real fix is owning the code that runs in your CI pipelines. Or fork the action itself and maintain it as part of your infrastructure.
在 HN 中查看
zanie 2026/04/09 20:16
我们确实在文章中解决了这个问题！这是纵深防御，而不是战区防御。

我们审核所有操作，检查它们是否引入可变依赖项，贡献上游修复，并尽可能使用任何操作进行迁移。

（我在星界工作）

We do address this in the article! It's defense in depth, not theater.

We audit all of our actions, check if they pull in mutable dependencies, contribute upstream fixes, and migrate off using any action when we can.

(I work at Astral)

在 HN 中查看
pojzon 2026/04/10 04:37
您是否分叉它们并有团队维护分叉？

如果不是，你就无法确定任何事情。它只是一个安全剧院。

Do you fork them and have a team maintaining forks ?

If not you cant be sure of anything. Its just a security theater.

在 HN 中查看
MeetingsBrowser 2026/04/09 21:28
> 它实际上是一个安全剧院。

我不同意。安全始终是一种权衡。

拥有、审计和维护整个供应链堆栈比固定哈希值更安全，但对于大多数项目来说并不实用。

固定哈希值比不固定更安全，并且接近免费。

最终，信任线会在某个地方划定（您是否审核 GitHub 提供的操作？）。在某个阶段，如果不信任某些第三方，就不可能编写和发布软件。

重要的是认识到你的“信任点”在哪里，并有意识地决定什么是值得你自己做的。

> It is effectively security theatre.

I disagree. Security is always a trade-off.

Owning, auditing, and maintaining your entire supply chain stack is more secure than pinning hashes, but it is not practical for most projects.

Pinning your hashes is more secure than not pinning, and is close to free.

At the end of the day, the line of trust is drawn somewhere (do you audit the actions provided by GitHub?). It is not possible to write and release software without trusting some third party at some stage.

The important part is recognizing where your "points of trust" are, and making a conscious decision about what is worth doing yourself.

在 HN 中查看
codethief 2026/04/09 19:25
无论如何，您不应该总是阅读并仔细检查您使用的第 3 方 GitHub 操作吗？ （仅分叉或复制其代码并不能解决您提到的问题，就像固定 SHA 无法解决的问题一样。）
Shouldn't you always read & double-check the 3rd-party GitHub actions you use, anyway? (Forking or copying their code alone doesn't solve the issue you mention any more than pinning a SHA does.)
在 HN 中查看
s_ting765 2026/04/09 20:03
仔细检查 Github 操作并不能减轻供应链漏洞带来的威胁。分叉一个操作会将信任从随机开发人员转移到您自己身上。您仍然必须确保该操作从可信来源拉取依赖项，这些来源也可以是您自己，具体取决于您想要走多远。
Double checking Github actions does not mitigate threats from supply chain vulnerabilities. Forking an action moves the trust from a random developer to yourself. You still have to make sure the action is pulling in dependencies from trusted sources which can also be yourself depending on how far you want to go.
在 HN 中查看
darkamaul 2026/04/09 13:34
鉴于最近影响 Trivy 和 litellm 的事件，我发现获得有关如何确保发布过程安全的指南非常有用。

这里的建议非常可靠且可操作，我建议任何团队阅读它们，并在可能的情况下实施它们。

供应链安全的可怕之处在于，我们的安全性取决于我们的依赖项，如果您使用的平台具有非安全默认设置，那么保护整个供应链的努力就会高得多。

With the recent incidents affecting Trivy and litellm, I find it extremely useful to have a guide on what to do to secure your release process.

The advices here are really solid and actionable, and I would suggest any team to read them, and implement them if possible.

The scary part with supply chain security is that we are only as secure as our dependencies, and if the platform you’re using has non secure defaults, the efforts to secure the full chain are that much higher.

kdeldycke 2026/04/09 23:42
我维护 `repomatic`，一个 Python CLI + 可重用工作流程。它将本文中的大部分实践融入到 Python 项目的嵌入式设置中（基于 uv，但也适用于其他项目）。目标是使安全默认设置成为只想发送包的维护人员的简单默认设置。还解决了 GitHub Actions 自身的很多缺点。

但多亏了这篇文章，我为 fork PR 工作流程审批策略添加了新的检查。

更多内容请见： https://github.com/kdeldycke/repomatic

I maintain `repomatic`, a Python CLI + reusable workflows. It bakes most of the practices from this post into a drop-in setup for Python projects (uv-based, but works for others too). The goal is to make the secure default the easy default for maintainers who just want to ship packages. Also addresses a lot of GitHub Actions own shortcomings.

But thanks to the article I added a new check for the fork PR workflow approval policy.

More at: https://github.com/kdeldycke/repomatic

在 HN 中查看
Eufrat 2026/04/09 15:08
Nix 提供声明式、可重复的构建。因此，表面上，如果您的构建系统使用 Nix，那么这里的一些问题就会消失。

不幸的是，Nix 也不是大多数人的运作方式。你必须按照尼克斯的方式做事，就这样。其价值部分来自于这种强烈的观点，但这也使其本质上是利基市场。大多数人不想仅仅为了获得此功能而学习全新的语言/范式。所以这就变成了先有鸡还是先有蛋的问题。恕我直言，我认为它还受到了一点势利和糟糕的命名（Nix vs. NixOS vs. Nixpkgs）的影响，这使得它更难获得关注。

Nix provides declarative, reproducible builds. So, ostensibly, if you had your build system using Nix, then some of the issues here go away.

Unfortunately, Nix is also not how most people function. You have to do things the Nix way, period. The value in part comes from this strong opinion, but it also makes it inherently niche. Most people do not want to learn an entire new language/paradigm just so they can get this feature. And so it becomes a chicken and egg problem. IMHO, I think it also suffers from a little bit of snobbery and poor naming (Nix vs. NixOS vs. Nixpkgs) which makes it that much harder to get traction.

在 HN 中查看
diffeomorphism 2026/04/09 15:33
“可再现”有不同的概念。 Nix 不会自动使构建以此处重要的方式重现：

https://reproducible.nixos.org

它仍然擅长这一点，但与其他发行版的差异相当小：

https://reproducible-builds.org/citests/

There are different notions of "reproducible". Nix does not automatically make builds reproducible in the way that matters here:

https://reproducible.nixos.org

It is still good at that but the difference to other distros is rather small:

https://reproducible-builds.org/citests/

在 HN 中查看
trashcan2137 2026/04/09 16:26
Nix，如果使用不正确（而且它们确实使使用它变得困难，无论正确还是错误，哈哈），都会为您提供可重复且可验证的构建。

不幸的是，我必须同意兄弟的评论，即它的命名不当，而且文档很难理解，这使得它更难获得关注。

我真的很讨厌 `it's all sales at the end of the day` 的想法，但如果 Nix 能够弄清楚如何将自己“推销”给更多人，那么我们可能会遇到更少的问题。

Nix, if not used incorrectly (and they really make it hard to use it, both correctly and incorrectly lol), gives you reproducible and verifiable builds.

Unfortunately I have to agree with the sibling comment that it suffers from poor naming and the docs are very hard to grok which makes it harder to get traction.

I really hate the idea of `it's all sales at the end of the day` but if Nix could figure how to "sell" itself to more people then we would probably have less of those problems.

在 HN 中查看
Zopieux 2026/04/09 16:52
关于使用哈希进行版本固定（以及使用工作流中的二进制 deps 的映射查找）的整个段落提醒我，软件工程师永远注定要重新发明更糟糕的 nixpkg 和 flake 版本。

我什至不喜欢 Nix，它充满了陷阱和怪异，但它提供了如此多的默认不变性和可重复性，以至于我有时会忘记每次供应链攻击成为新闻时其他人需要如何从第一原理重新发现这些东西。

The entire paragraph about version pinning using hashes (and using a map lookup for in-workflow binary deps) reminds me that software engineers are forever doomed to reinvent worse versions of nixpkgs and flakes.

I don't even love Nix, it's full of pitfalls and weirdnesses, but it provides so much by-default immutability and reproducibility that I sometimes forget how others need to rediscover this stuff from first principles every time a supply chain attack makes the news.
